(Romanian: [[tʃe̯ahˈlə.ul ˈpjatra ˈne̯amts]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")) or simply as **Ceahlăul**, is a Romanian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [club](/wiki/Club_(organization) "Club (organization)") based in [Piatra Neamț](/wiki/Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "Piatra Neamț"), [Neamț County](/wiki/Neam%C8%9B_County "Neamț County"), currently playing in the [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II").

**Club Sportiv Municipal Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț**, commonly known as **Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț** (Romanian: [[tʃe̯ahˈlə.ul ˈpjatra ˈne̯amts]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")) or simply as **Ceahlăul**, is a Romanian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [club](/wiki/Club_(organization) "Club (organization)") based in [Piatra Neamț](/wiki/Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "Piatra Neamț"), [Neamț County](/wiki/Neam%C8%9B_County "Neamț County"), currently playing in the [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II").

Originally established in 1919, the team's name stems from the nearby [Ceahlău Massif](/wiki/Ceahl%C4%83u_Massif "Ceahlău Massif"). *Nemțenii* made their first appearance in the [Romanian top division](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") in the [1993–94 season](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_Divizia_A "1993–94 Divizia A").

The club plays its home matches in black and yellow kits at the 18,000-seater [Stadionul Ceahlăul](/wiki/Stadionul_Ceahl%C4%83ul "Stadionul Ceahlăul").

### Early years (1919–1961)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years (1919–1961)")]

Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț was founded on 20 October 1919 in the town of [Piatra Neamț](/wiki/Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "Piatra Neamț"). The squad of high-school players was strengthened with soldiers from the 15th Infantry Regiment who had returned from the [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). In 1927, most of the club's players went to college and Ceahlăul went into a period of decline. It played in [Divizia C](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III") during the 1937–38 season, but left the league due to financial problems.[[1]](#cite_note-istoriafotbalului.ro-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1960-1961.jpg/330px-Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1960-1961.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1960-1961.jpg)

The 1960–61 team, which was promoted to Divizia B for the first time in club history

Ceahlăul revived in 1947 with a team which included Vulovici, Bălănescu, Ciciuc (Popovici), Actis, Manoliu, Dăscălescu, Vasiliu, Georgescu, Mata, Butnaru and Chiper.[[1]](#cite_note-istoriafotbalului.ro-1) The 1950s were marked by instability, with changes to the team's name, structure and players. Piatra Neamț had three football clubs: Avântul (the present-day Ceahlăul), Hârtia, and Celuloza. All had mixed results, generally in the lower leagues. In 1956, Avântul, Hârtia and Celuloza merged to form Recolta Piatra Neamț. Recolta played one season in the 12-team [Divizia C](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III"), finishing 11th. At the end of the season Recolta divided into two teams: Avântul and Rapid. After a poor season, the teams re-merged in 1958 to form CS Piatra Neamț. The team finished third in the 1958–59 Divizia C season.[[1]](#cite_note-istoriafotbalului.ro-1)[[2]](#cite_note-liga3-2) CS Piatra Neamț, coached by Tiberiu Căpăţînă, was promoted at the end of the 1960–61 season to [Divizia B](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") for the first time in club history.

### Three decades in the second league (1961–1993)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Three decades in the second league (1961–1993)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1964-1965.jpg/330px-Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1964-1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1964-1965.jpg)

The 1964–65 team, which was promoted back to [Divizia B](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") after one season in the third league

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1967.jpg/330px-Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1967.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1967.jpg)

Ceahlăul squad in 1967

During the summer of 1961, the club also changed its name back to Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț.[[1]](#cite_note-istoriafotbalului.ro-1) It played three consecutive seasons in Divizia B, finishing eighth in 1961–62, 10th in 1962–63 and 13th in 1963–64.[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3) Relegated to Divizia C, Ceahlăul was promoted back after [one season](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_Divizia_C "1964–65 Divizia C"); the club finished first, four points ahead of second-place [Textila Buhuși](/wiki/Textila_Buhu%C8%99i "Textila Buhuși").[[2]](#cite_note-liga3-2)

It played for many years in the second league, except for a Divizia C season in 1979–1980. Coached by [Petre Steinbach](/wiki/Petre_Steinbach "Petre Steinbach"), the team finished seventh in 1965–66, ninth in 1966–67, and fifth in 1967–68 and 1968–69.[[4]](#cite_note-4) After Steinbach's departure, the team finished 12th in 1969–70, eighth in 1970–71, 10th in 1971–72, 13th in 1972–73 and sixth in 1973–74.[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3)

Coached by Al Constantinescu, Ceahlăul finished fourth in 1974–75 – one point behind third-place [Gloria Buzău](/wiki/SCM_Gloria_Buz%C4%83u "SCM Gloria Buzău") and four points behind second-place [Progresul Brăila](/wiki/ACS_Dacia_Unirea_Br%C4%83ila "ACS Dacia Unirea Brăila").[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3) The team finished 13th in 1975–76, 10th in 1976–77 and 12th in 1977–78.

During the summer of 1978, the club changed its name from Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț to Relon Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț and had one of its weakest seasons since its last promotion in 1965. Finishing 15th out of 18 teams, it was relegated to Divizia C after 14 years in the second league;[[1]](#cite_note-istoriafotbalului.ro-1) six points separated Divizia B fourth-place [FC Constanța](/wiki/SSC_Farul_Constan%C8%9Ba "SSC Farul Constanța") from 17th-place [Victoria Tecuci](/wiki/Club_Sportiv_Sporting_Tecuci "Club Sportiv Sporting Tecuci").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Relon Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț changed its name back to Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț during the summer of 1979.[[1]](#cite_note-istoriafotbalului.ro-1) Ceahlăul finished first, seven points ahead of second-place [Foresta Fălticeni](/wiki/Foresta_F%C4%83lticeni "Foresta Fălticeni"), and was promoted back to the second league after one season in Divizia C.[[2]](#cite_note-liga3-2)

Coached by [Dumitru Dumitriu](/wiki/Dumitru_Dumitriu "Dumitru Dumitriu"), the team finished ninth in 1980–81, 10th in 1981–82 and seventh in 1982–83. Dumitriu was replaced by V. Copil in 1983, and the team finished fourth (a club record at the time) in 1983–84.[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3) Ceahlăul finished 11th in 1984–85 and 1985–86, eighth in 1986–87, ninth in 1987–88 and 11th in 1988–89. The team finished 14th in 1989–90 during the [Romanian Revolution](/wiki/Romanian_Revolution "Romanian Revolution"), just avoiding relegation.[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3)

[![A color team photo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1992-1993.jpg/250px-Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1992-1993.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_1992-1993.jpg)

The 1992–93 team, which was promoted to [Divizia A](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") for the first time in club history

The 1989 revolution was the beginning of the end for teams such as [Victoria București](/wiki/Victoria_Bucure%C8%99ti "Victoria București"), [Flacăra Moreni](/wiki/CSM_Flac%C4%83ra_Moreni "CSM Flacăra Moreni") and [Olt Scornicești](/wiki/FC_Olt_Scornice%C8%99ti "FC Olt Scornicești"), but was a restart for Ceahlăul. Long a mediocre Divizia B team, the yellow-and-blacks finished third in 1990–91 (three points out of second) and 1991–92 (three points behind second-place [FC Baia Mare](/wiki/FC_Minerul_Baia_Mare "FC Minerul Baia Mare") and 10 points ahead of Metrom Brașov.[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3)

[Ioan Sdrobiș](/wiki/Ioan_Sdrobi%C8%99 "Ioan Sdrobiș") ("The Father"), a coach known for promoting young players, was hired as manager during the summer of 1992. Gheorghe Ștefan became the club president, and [FC Argeș](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești"), [ASA Târgu Mureș](/wiki/ASA_T%C3%A2rgu_Mure%C8%99_(1962) "ASA Târgu Mureș (1962)"), [Gloria Buzău](/wiki/SCM_Gloria_Buz%C4%83u "SCM Gloria Buzău") and [Politehnica Iași](/wiki/FC_Politehnica_Ia%C8%99i_(1945) "FC Politehnica Iași (1945)") were rivals for promotion. Two teams were related to the former political regime: Steaua Mizil (a [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București") satellite team) and [Flacăra Moreni](/wiki/CSM_Flac%C4%83ra_Moreni "CSM Flacăra Moreni"). During the winter break, Sdrobiș left the team in first place after disputes with Ștefan and signed with [Dacia Unirea Brăila](/wiki/ACS_Dacia_Unirea_Br%C4%83ila "ACS Dacia Unirea Brăila"). He was replaced by former [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") and [Fenerbahçe](/wiki/Fenerbah%C3%A7e_S.K._(football) "Fenerbahçe S.K. (football)") player [Ion Nunweiller](/wiki/Ion_Nunweiller "Ion Nunweiller"). The yellow-and-blacks were promoted to [Divizia A](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") for the first time in club history with 20 victories, seven draws, seven losses, 54 goals scored and 24 conceded for 47 points, six points ahead of Steaua Mizil, [FC Argeș](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești") and [Flacăra Moreni](/wiki/CSM_Flac%C4%83ra_Moreni "CSM Flacăra Moreni"). The team consisted of coaches Sdrobiș (matches 1–17) and Nunweiller (matches 18–34) and players Anghelinei, [Șoiman](/wiki/Vasile_%C8%98oiman "Vasile Șoiman"), [Axinia I](/wiki/Gheorghe_Axinia "Gheorghe Axinia"), [Axinia II](/wiki/Florin_Axinia "Florin Axinia"), Dinu, Alexa, Cozma, Gălan, Coșerariu, [Enache](/wiki/Costel_Enache "Costel Enache"), Bârcă, Ghioane, Grosu, Ivanov, [Gigi Ion](/wiki/Gigi_Ion "Gigi Ion"), [Ionescu](/wiki/Mihai_Dan_Ionescu "Mihai Dan Ionescu"), [Lefter](/wiki/Radu_Lefter "Radu Lefter"), [Ov. Marc](/wiki/Ovidiu_Marc "Ovidiu Marc"), Mirea, Nichifor, Pantazi, Săvinoiu, Șoimaru, Urzică, Buliga, Oprea, Breniuc, Apachiței and Vrânceanu; the administrative leadership was ensured by: Gheorghe Ștefan, Gh. Chivorchian, Iulian Țocu, Liviu Tudor, Ioan Strătilă and Luigi Bodo.[[6]](#cite_note-6) For six months in 1993, it was known as Ceahlăul Simpex for sponsorship reason.[[1]](#cite_note-istoriafotbalului.ro-1)
Contributions to the team during this period were also made by I. Iovicin, M. Crețu, Radu Toma, V. Rizea, Tr. Coman, N. Zaharia, T. Anghelini, Toader Șteț, M. Nedelcu, M. Radu and Fl. Hizo (coaches) and C. Acatincăi, Gh. Ocneanu, D. Lospa and M. Contardo (presidents).[[1]](#cite_note-istoriafotbalului.ro-1)

### Golden age (1993–2004)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Golden age (1993–2004)")]

[![Ion Nunweiller as a player](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Ion_Nunweiller_%281971%29.jpg/250px-Ion_Nunweiller_%281971%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ion_Nunweiller_(1971).jpg)

[Ion Nunweiller](/wiki/Ion_Nunweiller "Ion Nunweiller") *(pictured in 1971)*, who coached the club to its first [Divizia A](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") promotion in 1993

At the start of the [1993–94 Divizia A](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_Divizia_A "1993–94 Divizia A") season, Ceahlăul was a well-known second-league team but largely unknown to most of Romania's first-division teams. With former player Mircea Nedelcu as the new coach, the team finished halfway down the table in 10th place. It finished fifth at the end of the [1994–95](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Divizia_A "1994–95 Divizia A") season and qualified for a European Cup.[[7]](#cite_note-liga1-7)

The club played in the [1995 UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/1995_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "1995 UEFA Intertoto Cup"), winning a group consisting of [FC Groningen](/wiki/FC_Groningen "FC Groningen"), [Beveren](/wiki/K.S.K._Beveren "K.S.K. Beveren"), [Boby Brno](/wiki/FC_Zbrojovka_Brno "FC Zbrojovka Brno") and [Etar Veliko Tarnovo](/wiki/FC_Etar_(Veliko_Tarnovo) "FC Etar (Veliko Tarnovo)") (2–0 against Etar, 2–0 against Beveren, 2–0 against Brno and 0–0 against Groningen). In the round of 16, the *nemțenii* lost to [FC Metz](/wiki/FC_Metz "FC Metz") of France 0–2, with goals scored by [Jocelyn Blanchard](/wiki/Jocelyn_Blanchard "Jocelyn Blanchard") and [Franck Meyrignac](/wiki/Franck_Meyrignac "Franck Meyrignac").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The club did less well during the regular season, finishing 15th (six points from the relegation zone).

Under coach [Florin Marin](/wiki/Florin_Marin "Florin Marin"), the yellow-and-blacks revived in the [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Divizia_A "1996–97 Divizia A") season to finish sixth. [Florin Marin](/wiki/Florin_Marin "Florin Marin"), Mircea Nedelcu, [Nicolae Manea](/wiki/Nicolae_Manea "Nicolae Manea") and [Viorel Hizo](/wiki/Viorel_Hizo "Viorel Hizo") coached the team to two consecutive ninth-place finishes in [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Divizia_A "1997–98 Divizia A") and [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Divizia_A "1998–99 Divizia A"). Ceahlăul prepared for the [1999 UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/1999_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "1999 UEFA Intertoto Cup"), eliminating two teams: [Ekranas](/wiki/FK_Ekranas "FK Ekranas") (2–0 on aggregate) and [Jedinstvo Bihać](/wiki/NK_Jedinstvo_Biha%C4%87 "NK Jedinstvo Bihać") (5–2 on aggregate). In the third round, Ceahlăul played two home-and-away matches against [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC"). The team drew 1–1 at Piatra Neamț, with goals by Scânteie in the 28th minute and [Alessio Tacchinardi](/wiki/Alessio_Tacchinardi "Alessio Tacchinardi") in the 58th. In Italy, at [Dino Manuzzi Stadium](/wiki/Stadio_Dino_Manuzzi "Stadio Dino Manuzzi") in [Cesena](/wiki/Cesena "Cesena"), they played a scoreless draw and were eliminated.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Ceahlăul finished fourth in Divizia A for the [best performance](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Divizia_A "1999–2000 Divizia A") in club history. Coached by Viorel Hizo, its players were [Eugen Anghel](/wiki/Eugen_Anghel "Eugen Anghel"), [Costel Câmpeanu](/wiki/Costel_C%C3%A2mpeanu "Costel Câmpeanu"), [Radu Lefter](/wiki/Radu_Lefter "Radu Lefter") – [Angelo Alistar](/wiki/Angelo_Alistar "Angelo Alistar"), Cristinel Atomulesei, [Adrian Baldovin](/wiki/Adrian_Baldovin "Adrian Baldovin"), [Dumitru Botez](/wiki/Dumitru_Botez "Dumitru Botez"), [Codruț Domșa](/wiki/Codru%C8%9B_Dom%C8%99a "Codruț Domșa"), [Costel Enache](/wiki/Costel_Enache "Costel Enache"), [Leontin Grozavu](/wiki/Leontin_Grozavu "Leontin Grozavu"), [Constantin Ilie](/wiki/Constantin_Ilie "Constantin Ilie"), [Mihai Dan Ionescu](/wiki/Mihai_Dan_Ionescu "Mihai Dan Ionescu"), [Ovidiu Marc](/wiki/Ovidiu_Marc "Ovidiu Marc"), [Mihai Nemțanu](/wiki/Mihai_Nem%C8%9Banu "Mihai Nemțanu"), Gheorghe Pantazi, [Dănuț Perjă](/wiki/D%C4%83nu%C8%9B_Perj%C4%83 "Dănuț Perjă"), [Daniel Scînteie](/wiki/Daniel_Sc%C3%AEnteie "Daniel Scînteie"), [Adrian Solomon](/wiki/Adrian_Solomon "Adrian Solomon"), [Tiberiu Șerban](/wiki/Tiberiu_%C8%98erban "Tiberiu Șerban"), Tudorel Șoimaru and [Lavi Hrib](/wiki/Lavi_Hrib "Lavi Hrib").

The 1999–2000 squad

[![Viorel Hizo, holding up a prize check in 1994](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ad/Viorel_Hizo%2C_1994_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viorel_Hizo,_1994_(cropped).jpg)

Coach [Viorel Hizo](/wiki/Viorel_Hizo "Viorel Hizo"), who obtained Ceahlăul's best results

In 2000, Ceahlăul again played in the [UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/2000_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "2000 UEFA Intertoto Cup"). After a 9–4 aggregate score against Estonia's [Narva Trans](/wiki/JK_Narva_Trans "JK Narva Trans") in the first round, the team defeated Spain's [Mallorca](/wiki/RCD_Mallorca "RCD Mallorca") 4–3. In the third round, the *nemțenii* drew 2–2 in Piatra Neamț against [Austria Wien](/wiki/FK_Austria_Wien "FK Austria Wien") before losing 0–3 at [Franz Horr Stadium](/wiki/Franz_Horr_Stadium "Franz Horr Stadium") in [Wien](/wiki/Wien "Wien") with goals scored by [Wagner](/wiki/Michael_Wagner_(footballer,_born_1975) "Michael Wagner (footballer, born 1975)"), Leitner and [Dospel](/wiki/Ernst_Dospel "Ernst Dospel").

During the early 2000s, coaching changes (11 in four years, including from Mircea Nedelcu to [Florin Halagian](/wiki/Florin_Halagian "Florin Halagian") and from [Florin Marin](/wiki/Florin_Marin "Florin Marin") to [Marin Barbu](/wiki/Marin_Barbu "Marin Barbu"), Viorel Hizo and [Marius Lăcătuș](/wiki/Marius_L%C4%83c%C4%83tu%C8%99 "Marius Lăcătuș")) led to poor results: 11th place in [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Divizia_A "2000–01 Divizia A"), eighth in [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Divizia_A "2001–02 Divizia A"), and 14th in [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Divizia_A "2003–04 Divizia A"). Ceahlăul was relegated in 2004 after 11 years and 10 seasons in the first league, with Hizo coaching the first half and Lăcătuș the second half.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The team finished fifth in [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Divizia_A "2002–03 Divizia A") and again played in the [UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/2003_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "2003 UEFA Intertoto Cup"), eliminated in the first round by [Tampere United](/wiki/Tampere_United "Tampere United") of Finland.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Club president Gheorghe Ștefan (nicknamed "Pinalti" because of his demands for a penalty in [Moldavian](/wiki/Moldavian_dialect "Moldavian dialect")) was accused of conspiring with Jean "Tata Jean" Pădureanu (the Gloria Bistrița president), father of the "Football Cooperative": a group of teams known for match-fixing in the 1990s. In addition to Ceahlăul, teams such as [Gloria Bistrița](/wiki/ACF_Gloria_Bistri%C8%9Ba "ACF Gloria Bistrița"), [FC Brașov](/wiki/SR_Bra%C8%99ov "SR Brașov"), [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București") and [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") were involved. Despite the lack of an official investigation, statements by players, coaches, and presidents and eyewitness reports attest to strange matches during the period. "Reciprocities" included sharing points to win a championship, qualifying for the European Cups or avoiding relegation. Pressure on referees gave the city (and Ceahlăul) the nickname "[Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War")".[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Another incident during "Ștefan's era" at Piatra Neamț occurred in 2000, before the first match of the third round of the [UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup") against [Austria Wien](/wiki/FK_Austria_Wien "FK Austria Wien"), when the president tried to offer prostitutes to the match referees; the club was suspended for a year from [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") competitions, but "Pinalti" said that the girls were members of a folk ensemble.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The first signs of the "Football Cooperative" were noted in 1993, when it was suspected that Ceahlăul offered suitcases with money for teams in the first series to pull hard against opponents or ease up as desired. Gelu Crăcană, a passionate supporter of the team and a member of its entourage, said in 2016: *"Mergeam cu genţi cu bani în majoritatea deplasărilor. Am mai cărat şi eu genţile, ţin minte că am purtat banii la mine când am mers la Autobuzul București"* ("We went with money bags in most of the trips. I've also carried these bags; I remember that I took the money with me when we went to [Autobuzul București](/wiki/AFC_Rocar_Bucure%C8%99ti "AFC Rocar București")").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Iulian Țocu, a director of the club at that time, described how many matches had been fixed that season.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### ABBA period (2004–2016)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: ABBA period (2004–2016)")]

[![Oval orange-and-yellow team logo, with a stylized bear](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/f/fe/FC_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_logo.png)](/wiki/File:FC_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B_logo.png)

Ceahlăul logo from 2006 to 2016, featuring a Carpathian bear

During this time, Ceahlăul was known as an "ABBA team" (a nickname used in Romania for clubs that used to alternate the presences between the first and the second league). After relegation, [Florin Marin](/wiki/Florin_Marin "Florin Marin") was hired as the new coach. Although the squad kept most of its players, Ceahlăul finished in fifth place – 18 points from the promotion place (occupied by [FC Vaslui](/wiki/FC_Vaslui "FC Vaslui")) and 22 from the first relegation place, occupied by FC Ghimbav. [Marin Barbu](/wiki/Marin_Barbu "Marin Barbu") replaced Marin the following season; the team was promoted, seven points ahead of second-place [Forex Brașov](/wiki/FC_Forex_Bra%C8%99ov "FC Forex Brașov") and eight points ahead of [FC Brașov](/wiki/SR_Bra%C8%99ov "SR Brașov").[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3) The club changed its logo, replacing the black goat against the [Ceahlău Massif](/wiki/Ceahl%C4%83u_Massif "Ceahlău Massif") background with a Carpathian bear; orange became the primary color, and the team was nicknamed *"Urșii carpatini"* ("The Carpathian Bears").

With many managerial changes and uninspired play, Ceahlăul finished the [2006–07 Liga I](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Liga_I "2006–07 Liga I") season in 15th place and was relegated. During the summer of 2007, however, [Delta Tulcea](/wiki/FC_Delta_Dobrogea_Tulcea "FC Delta Dobrogea Tulcea") (second place in [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II")) could not obtain a license for the upcoming Liga I season and Ceahlăul was accepted in its place.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Coached by Hizo, the club was relegated after again finishing in 15th place.

With Marin again at the helm of a new generation which included players such as [Andrei Vițelaru](/wiki/Andrei_Vi%C8%9Belaru "Andrei Vițelaru"), [Alexandru Forminte](/wiki/Alexandru_Forminte "Alexandru Forminte"), [Alexandru Ichim](/wiki/Alexandru_Ichim "Alexandru Ichim"), [Daniel Barna](/wiki/Daniel_Barna "Daniel Barna"), [Andrei Țepeș](/wiki/Andrei_%C8%9Aepe%C8%99 "Andrei Țepeș"), [Vlad Achim](/wiki/Vlad_Achim "Vlad Achim"), [Eugeniu Cebotaru](/wiki/Eugeniu_Cebotaru "Eugeniu Cebotaru"), [Vlad Achim](/wiki/Vlad_Achim "Vlad Achim"), [Ionuț Bădescu](/wiki/Ionu%C8%9B_B%C4%83descu "Ionuț Bădescu") or [Cristinel Gafița](/wiki/Cristinel_Gafi%C8%9Ba "Cristinel Gafița"), Ceahlăul was promoted back to [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") with 69 points (22 victories, three draws, five losses, 52 goals scored and 17 allowed. Second-place [CSM Ploiești](/wiki/FC_Astra_Giurgiu "FC Astra Giurgiu"), two points behind, was also promoted.[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3)

The Carpathian Bears were relegated for the third time in six years, finishing 17th out of 18 with 28 points in 34 matches. The club began the season with [Florin Marin](/wiki/Florin_Marin "Florin Marin"), continued with [Gheorghe Mulțescu](/wiki/Gheorghe_Mul%C8%9Bescu "Gheorghe Mulțescu"), and ended with ex-[Benfica](/wiki/S.L._Benfica "S.L. Benfica") player [Zoran Filipović](/wiki/Zoran_Filipovi%C4%87 "Zoran Filipović").[[7]](#cite_note-liga1-7)

[![Eugeniu Cebotaru in 2015, running after the ball](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/AUT_vs._MDA_2015-09-05_%28167%29.jpg/250px-AUT_vs._MDA_2015-09-05_%28167%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:AUT_vs._MDA_2015-09-05_(167).jpg)

[Eugeniu Cebotaru](/wiki/Eugeniu_Cebotaru "Eugeniu Cebotaru") played in 136 matches and scored 32 goals for Ceahlăul between 2006 and 2011, and was captain of the 2011 promotion squad.

Ceahlăul was again promoted at the end of the [2010–11 Liga II](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Liga_II "2010–11 Liga II") season, this time with [Marin Barbu](/wiki/Marin_Barbu "Marin Barbu") as coach. The squad included [Andrei Dumitraș](/wiki/Andrei_Dumitra%C8%99 "Andrei Dumitraș"), [Andrei Marc](/wiki/Andrei_Marc "Andrei Marc") and [Sebastian Chitoșcă](/wiki/Sebastian_Chito%C8%99c%C4%83 "Sebastian Chitoșcă").[[3]](#cite_note-liga2-3) Due to its good financial situation, excellent training conditions, a combination of youth and experience and talented coaches such as [Costel Enache](/wiki/Costel_Enache "Costel Enache"), [Vasile Miriuță](/wiki/Vasile_Miriu%C8%9B%C4%83 "Vasile Miriuță") and [Constantin Ilie](/wiki/Constantin_Ilie "Constantin Ilie"), Ceahlăul remained in the first league for four years and finished 11th in [2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Liga_I "2011–12 Liga I"), 14th in [2012–13](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Liga_I "2012–13 Liga I"), ninth in [2013–14](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Liga_I "2013–14 Liga I") and 18th in [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Liga_I "2014–15 Liga I").

In 2014, the club began drowning in debt. Ștefan, who was the mayor of [Piatra Neamț](/wiki/Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "Piatra Neamț") between 2004 and 2014, began to have legal problems and was arrested.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Without his or the municipality's help, 55 percent of the club's shares were sold to Italian businessman [Angelo Massone](/wiki/Angelo_Massone "Angelo Massone") in December of that year. Massone brought a number of players from the lower Italian and Spanish leagues, and hired coaches such as [Zé Maria](/wiki/Z%C3%A9_Maria_(footballer,_born_1973) "Zé Maria (footballer, born 1973)") and [Vanja Radinović](/wiki/Vanja_Radinovi%C4%87 "Vanja Radinović") to no avail.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Returned to the [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II"), without money from TV rights, without the help of the municipality and with an owner who did not seem to invest in the team, the media called the club as "Massone's Camp".[[22]](#cite_note-22) With uncertain finances, unpaid players, six coaches changed in a season and a policy of transfers from the lower Italian and Spanish leagues, Ceahlăul ended the regular season in 12th place and barely avoided relegation. The club withdrew from the championship with a few matches before the end of the play-out round and was relegated to the county leagues.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
Its financial problems were unmanageable, and the club began dissolution in the spring of 2016.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### New beginning (2016 – present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: New beginning (2016 – present)")]

On 22 July 2016, the media reported that the club would be re-founded as CSM Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț. Its founders were Mihai Bătrânu and Cătălin Roca, owners of former team sponsors Moldocor and Ro Com Central Companies. The RIFIL Company, another sponsor represented by Luigi Bodo and Ioan Strătilă, has indicated support for the new team.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) On 17 August of that year, the new club was legally registered.[[27]](#cite_note-Founded-27)

Its logo was also changed, the black goat and [Ceahlău Massif](/wiki/Ceahl%C4%83u_Massif "Ceahlău Massif") returning to the foreground in a restyled form, and the club colors returned to the original yellow and black. The team was enrolled in [Liga V](/wiki/Liga_V "Liga V"), and Toader Șteț was hired as the new coach. The squad was formed from players who grew up at the Ceahlăul Football Academy, and after one season it was promoted to [Liga IV](/wiki/Liga_IV "Liga IV") after a first-place finish: 16 victories, no draws or losses, 136 goals scored and five conceded for 48 points (13 more than second-place Olimpia Grințieș.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In the [2017–18 Liga IV](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Liga_IV "2017–18 Liga IV") season, the *nemțenii* squad included Teodor Cîmpianu, Alexandru Smău, [Marius Rusu](/wiki/Marius_Rusu "Marius Rusu"), Andrei Apostol, Cristian Copoț-Barb and Andrei Mateiciuc. The team entered the winter break in first place, when Toader Șteț was replaced by Gabriel Rădulescu.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In June 2023, seven years after it was relegated, the team returned to [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II").

## Youth program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Youth program")]

Youth academy of Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț is the biggest and most successful in [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia"), over time from this academy going into big football players such as: [Vasile Avădanei](/wiki/Vasile_Av%C4%83danei "Vasile Avădanei"), [Florin Axinia](/wiki/Florin_Axinia "Florin Axinia"), [Mihai Bordeianu](/wiki/Mihai_Bordeianu "Mihai Bordeianu"), [Lucian Burdujan](/wiki/Lucian_Burdujan "Lucian Burdujan"), [Robert Căruță](/wiki/Robert_C%C4%83ru%C8%9B%C4%83 "Robert Căruță"), Gelu Chertic, Lidi Chertic [Sebastian Chitoșcă](/wiki/Sebastian_Chito%C8%99c%C4%83 "Sebastian Chitoșcă"), [Lucian Covrig](/wiki/Lucian_Covrig "Lucian Covrig"), [Marian Drăghiceanu](/wiki/Marian_Dr%C4%83ghiceanu "Marian Drăghiceanu"), [Costel Enache](/wiki/Costel_Enache "Costel Enache"), [Alexandru Forminte](/wiki/Alexandru_Forminte "Alexandru Forminte"), [Alexandru Ichim](/wiki/Alexandru_Ichim "Alexandru Ichim"), [Constantin Ilie](/wiki/Constantin_Ilie "Constantin Ilie"), [Mihai Dan Ionescu](/wiki/Mihai_Dan_Ionescu "Mihai Dan Ionescu"), [Andrei Marc](/wiki/Andrei_Marc "Andrei Marc"), [Florin Nohai](/wiki/Florin_Nohai "Florin Nohai"), [Doru Popadiuc](/wiki/Doru_Popadiuc "Doru Popadiuc"), Gabriel Rădulescu, [Adrian Solomon](/wiki/Adrian_Solomon "Adrian Solomon"), Tudor Șoimaru, [Andrei Țepeș](/wiki/Andrei_%C8%9Aepe%C8%99 "Andrei Țepeș") or [Andrei Vițelaru](/wiki/Andrei_Vi%C8%9Belaru "Andrei Vițelaru"). Among the most important talent finders in Ceahlăul Football Academy were professors Radu Toma and Mihai Radu.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Grounds")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Stadionul_Ceahl%C4%83ul_-_tribune_%283%29.jpg/250px-Stadionul_Ceahl%C4%83ul_-_tribune_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadionul_Ceahl%C4%83ul_-_tribune_(3).jpg)

Ceahlăul Stadium in 2013.

The club plays its home matches on [Stadionul Ceahlăul](/wiki/Stadionul_Ceahl%C4%83ul "Stadionul Ceahlăul") from [Piatra Neamț](/wiki/Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "Piatra Neamț"). Originally known as *Borzoghean* and still nicknamed in this mode by the supporters, the stadium had a capacity of 12,000 seats and the shape of letter "U". Between 2006 and 2007 the stadium was renovated, extended (a new End Sector was built), orange seats were mounted instead of the old yellow and black ones and a floodlight installation was also installed. The capacity reached 18,000 seats after other renovations which took place in the early 2010s. Stadionul Ceahlăul is a 3 star ranked in the [UEFA stadium categories](/wiki/UEFA_stadium_categories "UEFA stadium categories").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Support")]

Ceahlăul has many supporters in [Piatra Neamț](/wiki/Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "Piatra Neamț") and especially in [Neamț County](/wiki/Neam%C8%9B_County "Neamț County"). They are not exactly the ultras type, but despite this fact some ultras groups were formed over time such as: *Brigate Ultras 2009* and *Tinerii Nemțeni*.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 2010 "the Yellow and Blacks" supporters surprised everyone by the fact that they had the first and only ultras leader in Romania, who is a woman, Geanina Ciocoiu.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Ceahlăul does not have important rivalries, most of them being regional, the so-called *Derby-urile Moldovei* ([Moldavia Derbies](/wiki/Western_Moldavia "Western Moldavia")) against teams such as: [FCM Bacău](/wiki/FCM_Bac%C4%83u "FCM Bacău"), [FC Politehnica Iași](/wiki/FC_Politehnica_Ia%C8%99i_(2010) "FC Politehnica Iași (2010)") or [Oțelul Galați](/wiki/ASC_O%C8%9Belul_Gala%C8%9Bi "ASC Oțelul Galați") and most recently against [FC Vaslui](/wiki/FC_Vaslui "FC Vaslui") or [FC Botoșani](/wiki/FC_Boto%C8%99ani "FC Botoșani").

### Trivia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Trivia")]

In 2006, Gelu Crăcană, a fan of Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț created a 420 square meters (500 including the sleeves) Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț jersey shirt with [Florin Axinia](/wiki/Florin_Axinia "Florin Axinia")'s name on it which entered the [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records") Hall of fame.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)
